title: BBC English Conversations
description: Real-life English conversations from BBC Learning English — natural, everyday dialogue

episodes.title: At the Coffee Shop
episodes.description: Listen to a natural conversation between two friends catching up at a coffee shop

transcript.text: Hi Sarah! It's so good to see you. How have you been?
Tom! Great to see you too. I've been really busy with work lately.
Tell me about it. What have you been up to?
I just finished a big project. It took three months but we finally launched it last week.
That's amazing! You must be relieved. Are you celebrating?
Well, that's kind of why I wanted to meet. The team is going out for dinner tonight. Would you like to join us?
Oh, I'd love to! What kind of food are we having?
Italian. There's a new restaurant that just opened downtown. Apparently the pasta is incredible.
Sounds perfect. What time should I be there?
Seven o'clock. I'll send you the address. Oh, and smart casual dress code.
No problem. I'll be there. Now, what can I get you to drink?
A flat white, please. With oat milk if they have it.
Good choice. I think I'll have the same. The coffee here is really good.
Exactly. I discovered this place last month. It's become my regular spot.
Key phrase: 'my regular spot' means a place you go to often. A spot is an informal word for a place.

episodes.title: Job Application Phone Call
episodes.description: Hear how to handle a professional phone call about a job application in English

transcript.text: Good morning, Innovatech Solutions. How can I help you?
Good morning. My name is James Chen. I'm calling about the software developer position I applied for last week.
Of course, Mr. Chen. Let me pull up your application. Could you hold for just a moment?
Certainly, no problem.
Thank you for holding. I can see your application here. Our hiring team was impressed with your portfolio.
Thank you, I'm very excited about the opportunity to join your team.
We'd like to invite you for a first-round interview. Are you available this Thursday or Friday?
Friday would be ideal. Could you tell me the format? Will it be in person or online?
It will be a hybrid — a 30-minute video call with the technical team, followed by a brief in-person tour if things go well.
That sounds great. What time on Friday works best?
We have slots at ten in the morning or two in the afternoon.
Ten in the morning would be perfect, thank you.
Excellent. I'll send a confirmation email with all the details. Is the email address on your application still current?
Yes, james.chen at gmail dot com. That's correct.
Perfect. We look forward to speaking with you on Friday, Mr. Chen. Have a great day.
Thank you so much. I look forward to it as well. Goodbye.

episodes.title: Discussing the News
episodes.description: Two colleagues discuss a current news story during their lunch break

transcript.text: Did you read that article about the new climate agreement this morning?
Yes, I did. It's quite significant, isn't it? Over 190 countries signed it.
It is, but I'm a bit skeptical. I mean, how enforceable is it really?
That's a fair point. Skeptical means doubtful — not fully convinced something is true or will work.
Right. We've seen these agreements before. The Paris Agreement was promising, but many countries missed their targets.
True. But this one apparently has stronger accountability measures built in. Countries that miss targets face trade penalties.
That does change things. Economic consequences tend to get governments' attention.
Exactly. And there's independent monitoring this time — not just self-reporting.
Self-reporting is when countries measure and report their own progress. Independent monitoring means an outside body verifies it.
I suppose I'm cautiously optimistic then. The mechanism is stronger, even if the ambition isn't enough.
Cautiously optimistic — that's a useful phrase. It means hopeful but with some reservations.
What did the article say about renewable energy targets?
The goal is 60% renewable energy globally by 2040. That's ambitious compared to the current 30%.
It'll require enormous investment. Where's that money supposed to come from?
A mix of public funding and private sector investment, apparently. Green bonds are going to play a big role.

episodes.title: Negotiating a Pay Rise
episodes.description: Learn how to confidently negotiate a salary increase with your manager

transcript.text: Hi, do you have a moment? I wanted to talk about my performance review.
Of course, Emma. Come in and sit down. What's on your mind?
I've been here three years now, and I feel I've taken on a lot more responsibility this year.
That's true. Your work on the Harris account was exceptional — we were all very impressed.
Thank you. Given that, I'd like to discuss the possibility of a salary review.
Key phrase: 'discuss the possibility of' — a polite way to raise a sensitive request.
What figure were you thinking, Emma?
Based on industry benchmarks and my expanded role, I was hoping for a 12% increase.
Benchmark means a standard or point of reference for comparison.
That's higher than our standard review process, but I understand your reasoning.
I'm prepared to justify it with the client revenue I brought in — 40% above my target.
That's compelling data. I'll need to discuss it with HR and come back to you.
Compelling means convincing — strong enough to make someone consider it seriously.
Of course. When do you think you'd be able to give me an answer?
By end of next week. I want to make sure we can retain you — you're a key member of the team.
Retain means to keep someone — the opposite of losing an employee to another company.

episodes.title: Planning a Holiday
episodes.description: Two friends discuss and plan an upcoming holiday trip together

transcript.text: Right, so we finally agreed on Portugal! I'm so excited.
Same! Now we need to sort out the details. When were you thinking?
I was thinking late May — the weather's lovely and it's before the peak tourist season.
Peak season means the time of year when the most tourists visit — often more expensive and crowded.
Good thinking. How long should we go for?
At least a week. There's Lisbon, Porto, the Algarve — so much to see.
Should we book a package deal or sort everything separately?
A package deal includes flights and accommodation together — often cheaper than booking individually.
I'd rather be flexible and book separately. We can shop around for the best flights.
Shop around means to compare prices at different places before buying.
Fair enough. Should we stay in hotels or try an Airbnb? I like having a kitchen.
A mix? Hotel in Lisbon for the first few nights, then a rental apartment in Porto.
Perfect. I'll look at flights tonight. Shall we set a budget first?
Let's say £800 each all in — flights, accommodation, food and activities.
All in means including everything — the total cost of all expenses combined.

episodes.title: Renting a Flat
episodes.description: Navigate a conversation with a landlord when viewing and renting a flat

transcript.text: Good morning! You must be Daniel. I'm Mrs. Patel, the landlord. Come in.
Good morning, Mrs. Patel. Thank you for showing me round.
Of course. So this is the lounge — as you can see it's quite spacious and gets a lot of light in the morning.
Spacious means having plenty of space. The lounge is the sitting room or living room.
It's lovely. Are all the furnishings included, or is it unfurnished?
It's part-furnished — the sofas and beds are included but there's no dining table or wardrobe.
That works for me. What's the monthly rent?
£1,400 per calendar month, which includes water and council tax.
Council tax is a local tax paid to the local government for services like rubbish collection.
And what's the minimum tenancy?
Twelve months initially, with a two-month notice period after that.
A notice period is the time you must tell your landlord before you leave.
Is the deposit one month or six weeks?
Five weeks, as per the tenant fee act. It'll be held in a government-backed deposit scheme.
A deposit scheme protects your money — it's returned when you leave if no damage is found.
Great. I'm definitely interested. What's the application process?

episodes.title: Complaining Politely
episodes.description: How to complain effectively and politely in everyday English situations

transcript.text: Excuse me. I'm sorry to bother you, but I have a problem with my order.
Of course, sir. What seems to be the matter?
I ordered the salmon, but I've been served the chicken. I'm vegetarian, so I can't eat this.
Note the polite opener: 'I'm sorry to bother you' — this softens the complaint immediately.
I sincerely apologise for that. Let me take this back and get your salmon right away.
Thank you. Also, I just wanted to mention — the soup earlier was a little cold. Not a major issue, just for your feedback.
Useful phrase: 'just for your feedback' — this frames the complaint as helpful, not aggressive.
You're absolutely right, and I appreciate you letting us know. I'll pass that on to the kitchen.
Can I offer you a complimentary dessert for the inconvenience?
Complimentary means free of charge — given as a gift or apology.
That's very kind of you. Thank you very much.
Key tips: stay calm, be specific about the problem, and give the other person a chance to fix it.
Avoid phrases like 'This is unacceptable!' or 'I demand...' — they can escalate the situation.
Escalate means to make something more serious or intense.

episodes.title: Talking About the Future
episodes.description: Use different grammar structures to discuss plans, predictions and possibilities

transcript.text: So what are you going to do after you finish university?
I'm going to take a gap year and travel around Southeast Asia.
'Going to' is used for plans and intentions we have already decided on.
That sounds amazing! Will you go alone?
'Will' is used for predictions and spontaneous decisions made at the moment of speaking.
Probably not. I'm meeting a friend in Thailand. We're travelling together from there.
Using the present continuous for future — 'I'm meeting' — shows a fixed, confirmed arrangement.
I might go interrailing in Europe the year after. I haven't decided yet.
'Might' expresses possibility — something that may or may not happen.
You should plan it now! Trains book up fast in summer.
'Should' here gives advice or a recommendation.
You're probably right. I'll look into it.
'I'll look into it' is a common phrase meaning I will investigate or consider something.
Summary: going to = plan, will = prediction/spontaneous, might = possibility, present continuous = arrangement.
